name: Advanced IAM Configuration for Security
description: Implementing fine-grained IAM policies with conditions, deny policies, service account security, and Workforce Identity Federation for enterprise security.
key_facts: IAM deny policies explicitly deny permissions regardless of allow policies; evaluated before allow policies
IAM conditions use CEL expressions to restrict access by time, resource name, or request attributes
Workforce Identity Federation lets external IdP users (Okta, Azure AD) authenticate without Google accounts using OIDC/SAML
Service account impersonation (iam.serviceAccounts.actAs) allows a principal to act as a service account; audit this carefully
IAM Recommender uses ML to identify overly permissive roles and recommends least-privilege replacements
common_misconceptions: IAM deny policies and org policy constraints are the same — deny policies work at IAM permission level; org policies enforce resource configuration
Workforce Identity Federation is only for external users — it can also federate on-premises Active Directory users into GCP

name: VPC Service Controls and Access Context Manager
description: Creating security perimeters with VPC Service Controls to prevent data exfiltration from GCP services, using Access Levels for context-aware access.
key_facts: VPC Service Controls restricts API calls to and from protected services; even authorized identities cannot move data outside the perimeter
Service perimeters define which projects and services are inside; access policies define which identities can cross the perimeter
Access Context Manager defines access levels based on IP range, device policy, and user identity for context-aware access
Dry run mode logs what would be blocked without enforcing, enabling safe testing before perimeter enforcement
Perimeter bridges allow two separate perimeters to share data for approved use cases
common_misconceptions: VPC Service Controls protect against external hackers — they primarily prevent insider threats and data exfiltration; external access is still controlled by IAM and firewalls
All GCP services are supported by VPC Service Controls — support varies by service; check the supported services list before designing the perimeter

name: Cloud KMS and Encryption Key Management
description: Managing encryption keys with Cloud KMS, implementing CMEK for customer-controlled encryption, and using Cloud HSM for hardware-backed key security.
key_facts: Cloud KMS key hierarchy: Key Ring > Key (CryptoKey) > Key Version; primary version is used for encryption
CMEK (Customer-Managed Encryption Keys) lets customers control key lifecycle for at-rest encryption in BigQuery, Cloud Storage, and other services
Cloud HSM provides FIPS 140-2 Level 3 hardware security module protection for cryptographic keys
Key rotation: schedule automatic rotation; old versions retained for decryption but not encryption
CSEK (Customer-Supplied Encryption Keys): customer provides the key material; Google never stores it; key must be provided on every API call
common_misconceptions: CMEK prevents Google from accessing your data — CMEK controls the key, but Google's infrastructure still processes data; for higher isolation use Confidential VMs
Deleting a KMS key version is reversible — destroyed key versions are permanently deleted; any data encrypted with that version becomes unrecoverable

name: Security Command Center Threat Detection
description: Using Security Command Center Premium for threat detection, vulnerability scanning, compliance monitoring, and incident response on GCP.
key_facts: SCC Standard (free) provides security health analytics; Premium adds Event Threat Detection, Container Threat Detection, and Web Security Scanner
Findings are categorized by severity (Critical, High, Medium, Low) and source (SHA, ETD, CTD, custom)
Event Threat Detection uses Google threat intelligence to detect brute force, crypto mining, and data exfiltration in logs
Security Health Analytics identifies misconfigurations: public Cloud Storage buckets, weak firewall rules, unused service account keys
SCC integrates with SIEM tools via Pub/Sub export for Security Operations teams using Chronicle or Splunk
common_misconceptions: SCC is a firewall that blocks attacks — SCC is a detection and monitoring tool; it identifies risks but does not automatically block them
SCC Premium is only for large enterprises — any organization handling sensitive data benefits from Premium's threat detection

name: Cloud Armor WAF and DDoS Protection
description: Configuring Cloud Armor security policies to protect web applications from DDoS attacks, SQL injection, XSS, and other OWASP threats.
key_facts: Cloud Armor is a network security service attached to External Application Load Balancers at the global edge
Security policies contain rules with match conditions (IP ranges, geo, request headers, reCAPTCHA) and actions (allow, deny, rate-limit, redirect)
Preconfigured WAF rules implement OWASP Top 10 protections (SQLi, XSS, LFI, RFI, RCE) with sensitivity levels
Adaptive Protection uses ML to detect and automatically suggest rules to block DDoS attacks in near-real-time
Cloud Armor Managed Protection Plus provides higher DDoS protection SLA and dedicated support
common_misconceptions: Cloud Armor protects internal load balancers — Cloud Armor only attaches to External Application (HTTP/S) Load Balancers
IP allowlisting alone is sufficient security — attackers can spoof or compromise allowed IPs; use WAF rules and rate limiting too

name: VPC Firewall Rules and Firewall Policies
description: Designing and implementing VPC firewall rules and hierarchical firewall policies to control traffic within and between GCP networks.
key_facts: Firewall rules are stateful (connection tracking), evaluated by priority (0-65534), and scoped to a VPC network
Hierarchical firewall policies apply at organization or folder level and take precedence over VPC-level rules
Network firewall policies (replacing legacy rules) enable consistent firewall management and FQDN-based rules
Implied rules: deny all ingress and allow all egress; cannot be deleted but can be overridden by lower-priority rules
Service account-based firewall rules apply to VMs by service account rather than IP, providing identity-based network segmentation
common_misconceptions: Firewall rules block traffic to stopped VMs — firewall rules don't matter for stopped VMs; traffic reaches the VM only when it's running
Allowing all egress is safe — unrestricted egress enables data exfiltration and C2 communication; restrict outbound traffic where possible

name: Cloud Audit Logs and Compliance Monitoring
description: Configuring and analyzing Cloud Audit Logs (Admin Activity, Data Access, System Event, Policy Denied) for security monitoring and compliance requirements.
key_facts: Admin Activity logs are always enabled and cannot be disabled; record resource configuration and metadata changes
Data Access logs are disabled by default (except BigQuery); must be enabled explicitly per service for read/write data access
Cloud Audit Logs retention: Admin Activity (400 days), Data Access (30 days by default); use log sinks for longer retention
Log sinks to Cloud Storage, BigQuery, or Pub/Sub enable long-term retention and SIEM integration
Audit log analysis using BigQuery enables querying audit logs for compliance reporting and forensic investigation
common_misconceptions: All audit logging is enabled by default — only Admin Activity logs are on by default; Data Access logs must be explicitly enabled
Audit logs cannot be tampered with — audit logs should be exported to a locked Cloud Storage bucket with object versioning for tamper evidence

name: Data Loss Prevention and Sensitive Data Protection
description: Using Cloud DLP to inspect, classify, and de-identify sensitive data across storage systems and data pipelines to meet compliance requirements.
key_facts: Cloud DLP supports 150+ built-in InfoType detectors for PII (SSN, credit cards, email, phone), healthcare, and finance data
De-identification techniques: redaction, masking, tokenization, pseudonymization, format-preserving encryption
Inspection jobs scan Cloud Storage, BigQuery, and Datastore for sensitive data on a schedule
DLP API can be integrated inline in streaming pipelines (Dataflow) for real-time data classification
Risk analysis jobs calculate statistical metrics (k-anonymity, l-diversity) to assess re-identification risk
common_misconceptions: DLP completely eliminates privacy risk — DLP helps manage risk but re-identification is still possible with enough external data
Masking data makes it safe to share externally — masked data may still contain quasi-identifiers; apply proper anonymization

name: BeyondCorp Enterprise and Zero-Trust Access
description: Implementing zero-trust network access with BeyondCorp Enterprise for secure access to internal applications without a traditional VPN.
key_facts: BeyondCorp Enterprise grants access based on user identity and device security state, not network location
Identity-Aware Proxy (IAP) enforces application-level access control based on Google Identity and context-aware access levels
Device posture policies check endpoint attributes (OS version, antivirus, disk encryption) before granting access
BeyondCorp eliminates VPN by authenticating every request at the application layer regardless of source network
Access Context Manager integrates with BeyondCorp to define granular access levels based on IP, device, and geography
common_misconceptions: BeyondCorp and VPN provide the same security — BeyondCorp provides per-application, per-request authentication; VPN grants network-level access once authenticated
IAP only works for web applications — IAP also supports TCP tunneling for SSH and RDP access to GCP VMs

name: Compliance Frameworks and Assured Workloads
description: Implementing compliance controls for FedRAMP, HIPAA, PCI DSS, and GDPR using Assured Workloads, organization policies, and GCP compliance tools.
key_facts: Assured Workloads enforces compliance controls at the project level: restricts resource locations, encryption settings, and personnel access
FedRAMP compliance requires limiting data to US regions, using authorized services, and maintaining personnel access controls
HIPAA requires a BAA with Google, audit logging, access controls, and encryption for PHI data
PCI DSS requires network segmentation (VPC Service Controls), encryption, access logging, and vulnerability scanning
Forseti Security and Config Validator enforce policy-as-code to continuously audit for compliance drift
common_misconceptions: FedRAMP applies to all GCP services — only FedRAMP-authorized services can be used for federal workloads; check the authorized service list
Compliance is a one-time audit — compliance requires continuous monitoring and evidence collection, not just initial configuration

name: Private Networking and Google Private Access
description: Configuring Private Google Access, Private Service Connect, and Cloud NAT to eliminate external IPs while maintaining connectivity to Google APIs and services.
key_facts: Private Google Access allows VMs without external IPs to reach Google APIs using internal IP addresses
Private Service Connect provides VPC-native endpoints for Google services (Storage, BigQuery) within your VPC
Cloud NAT enables outbound internet access for VMs without external IPs without exposing them inbound
VPC-SC and Private Service Connect together prevent data exfiltration: private access + perimeter enforcement
Serverless VPC Access connector allows Cloud Run, Cloud Functions, and App Engine to access VPC resources privately
common_misconceptions: Removing external IPs from VMs eliminates all security risks — VMs without external IPs can still be reached via internal routes or compromised services
Private Google Access and Private Service Connect are identical — PGA uses routing; PSC creates VPC endpoints with internal IPs

name: Cloud Security Incident Response
description: Building cloud-native incident response capabilities including detection, investigation, containment, and forensics on Google Cloud.
key_facts: Chronicle SIEM provides petabyte-scale security telemetry analysis using Google infrastructure for threat hunting
Cloud Logging exports to Pub/Sub enable real-time log streaming to SIEM tools for automated alerting
VM snapshots capture disk state for forensic analysis; use snapshots before quarantining suspected instances
IAM policy revocation and service account key deletion contain access during an incident
Google Cloud's Shared Fate model means Google actively invests in customer security posture beyond just infrastructure
common_misconceptions: Deleting a compromised VM eliminates all evidence — take a disk snapshot first for forensic investigation before terminating
Cloud providers handle incident response for you — you are responsible for detecting and responding to incidents in your environment

name: Binary Authorization and Container Security
description: Implementing supply chain security for containerized workloads using Binary Authorization, Artifact Registry vulnerability scanning, and SLSA frameworks.
key_facts: Binary Authorization enforces a policy requiring attestations from authorized attestors before deploying to GKE or Cloud Run
Attestors cryptographically sign container image digests to certify they passed specific quality gates (security scan, code review)
Artifact Registry Container Analysis scans images for CVEs using OS and language package vulnerability databases
SLSA (Supply-chain Levels for Software Artifacts) levels 1-4 define provenance and build integrity requirements
Sigstore (Cosign) provides open-source container signing integrated with Binary Authorization attestations
common_misconceptions: Vulnerability scanning guarantees a container is safe to deploy — scanners have false negatives; scanning is one layer in a defense-in-depth strategy
Binary Authorization blocks runtime threats — it enforces deploy-time policy; use runtime security tools for post-deployment protection

name: Secret Manager for Secrets Lifecycle Management
description: Managing application secrets, API keys, and certificates using Secret Manager with versioning, rotation, replication, and access auditing.
key_facts: Secret Manager stores secrets as opaque blobs up to 64 KB; versions allow rotation without changing secret names
Automatic secret replication vs manual: automatic replicates to multiple regions; manual allows specifying CMEK per region
Secret rotation best practice: add new version, update applications, disable old version, then destroy after confirmation
Cloud Audit Logs tracks every access to secrets; integrate with SCC for alerting on unusual secret access patterns
Pub/Sub notifications on secret events (creation, rotation, expiry) enable automated rotation workflows
common_misconceptions: Disabling a secret version immediately prevents access — existing processes holding the secret in memory still have it; disable only stops new reads
Secret Manager and Cloud KMS serve the same purpose — KMS manages encryption keys; Secret Manager stores arbitrary secret values

name: Organization Policies for Security Governance
description: Using Organization Policy Service to enforce security guardrails across an entire GCP organization, including resource location restrictions and service restrictions.
key_facts: Organization policies are evaluated during resource creation and configuration; they do not affect existing resources
Key security policies: constraints/compute.requireOsLogin, constraints/iam.disableServiceAccountKeyCreation, constraints/compute.vmExternalIpAccess
Custom organization policies (Preview) use CEL to write policy conditions against resource attributes
Policy inheritance: organization > folder > project; child nodes can be overridden if parent allows exception
Policy intelligence tools: Organization Policy Analyzer and Policy Simulator validate policy impact before enforcement
common_misconceptions: Organization policies enforce IAM permissions — org policies constrain resource configuration; IAM controls identity-based access to operations
Org policies apply retroactively to existing resources — they only apply to new resource creation and updates, not pre-existing resources
